sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 35, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Sky blue background (node 0) fills most of the 64x64 board - White robot/character shape (node 2, 135 px) at upper-left area (rows ~11-25, cols ~11-25) with two red 3x3 "feet" blocks (nodes 3, 4) below it - Green rectangular target (node 5, 144 px) at lower-right (rows 29-49, cols 41-49) with a sky blue hollow center (node 6) - Two red 3x3 blocks (nodes 7, 8) positioned to the left of the green target - Charcoal bar (node 1) at top edge - likely HUD/timer
- Goal model: Move the white character to the green target area.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT likely move the character. Need to test.
- Plan: Try UP and DOWN to understand vertical movement, then figure out the completion condition.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 34 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
UP moved the character upward. Looking at the frames:
- Step 33: character was lower, green block at bottom
- Step 34: after UP, character moved up, green block moved up too
The white character (W, 135 px) moves and leaves a green (N) block behind. The green frame (N, 90 px) stays as a border. The red blocks (R) seem to be fixed obstacles or markers on the left and right sides.
Looking at the pattern:
- The green frame is a rectangular border
- The white character moves inside it
- The character leaves a green trail (the N blocks)
- The red blocks are obstacles on the sides
The goal is likely to fill the entire green frame area with green by moving the character through all positions. The character seems to paint green wherever it goes.
Let me look at the current state more carefully. The white character is at rows 32-46, cols 44-58. The green frame is at rows 29-49, cols 41-49. Wait, that doesn't match - the white character extends beyond the green frame's column range.
Let me re-examine. Looking at the segmentation:
- Node 3: N (light green), 90 px, r=29-49, c=41-49 - this is the green frame border
- Node 4: W (white), 135 px, r=32-46, c=44-58 - this is the character
- Node 6: N (light green), 9 px, r=35-37, c=47-49 - green trail block
- Node 8: S (sky blue), 18 px, r=41-46, c=44-46 - sky blue area inside frame
The character seems to be a 3D-like shape that extends to the right. The green frame is the target area. The goal might be to position the character so that the green trail fills the frame completely.
Let me try to explore more systematically. I'll try moving DOWN to see if the character can reach the bottom of the frame.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model update: UP moves the white character upward within the green frame. The character leaves green (N) blocks as a trail. The goal appears to be filling the green frame area completely by navigating through all positions. Red blocks are fixed obstacles on left/right sides.
Plan: Try DOWN to explore vertical range, then map out all reachable positions to find the completion pattern.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after UP
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

print("Before UP nodes:")
for n in before.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 500:
        b = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in b)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in b)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"  id={n['id']}: {n['color']} px={n['pixels']} r={min_r}-{max_r} c={min_c}-{max_c}")

print("\nAfter UP nodes:")
for n in after.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 500:
        b = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in b)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in b)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"  id={n['id']}: {n['color']} px={n['pixels']} r={min_r}-{max_r} c={min_c}-{max_c}")

# Try DOWN to return and see the pattern
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
